concept.title: Quantum Numbers
body: Four quantum numbers describe the unique state of an electron in an atom.
Principal quantum number $n$ ($n = 1,2,3,...$) determines the energy level (shell) and size of the orbital.
Azimuthal (angular momentum) quantum number $l$ ($l = 0$ to $n-1$) determines the shape of the orbital — $l=0$ is s, $l=1$ is p, $l=2$ is d, $l=3$ is f.
Magnetic quantum number $m_l$ ($m_l = -l$ to $+l$) describes the orientation of the orbital in space. Number of orbitals in a subshell $= 2l+1$.
Spin quantum number $m_s$ ($+\frac{1}{2}$ or $-\frac{1}{2}$) describes the spin direction of the electron.
Maximum electrons in a subshell $= 2(2l+1)$. For example, a d-subshell ($l=2$) holds a maximum of 10 electrons; an f-subshell ($l=3$) holds 14.

questions.question: The maximum number of electrons in a subshell with azimuthal quantum number $l = 3$ is:
questions.explanation: For $l=3$ (f-subshell), number of orbitals = $2l+1 = 7$. Max electrons = $2 \times 7 = 14$.

questions.question: Which quantum numbers are allowed for an electron in the 3d subshell? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: For 3d: $n=3$, $l=2$, and $m_l$ ranges from $-2$ to $+2$. $l=3$ is not allowed for $n=3$ (since $l < n$), and $m_l=+3$ exceeds the limit.

questions.question: What is the maximum number of electrons that can occupy the shell with $n = 3$?

concept.title: Chemical Bonding
body: Chemical bonds form when atoms share or transfer electrons to achieve a stable (often octet) electron configuration.

Bond order, calculated from Molecular Orbital Theory, equals half the difference between bonding and antibonding electrons: Bond Order = (Nb - Na)/2. Higher bond order generally means a shorter, stronger bond.

Polarity arises when there is unequal sharing of electrons (different electronegativities) combined with an asymmetric molecular geometry. Symmetric molecules like CO2 and CCl4 are non-polar even though individual bonds may be polar, because the bond dipoles cancel.
VSEPR theory predicts molecular geometry based on minimizing repulsion between electron pairs (bonding and lone pairs) around the central atom.
Common geometries: linear (2 electron domains), trigonal planar (3), tetrahedral (4), trigonal bipyramidal (5), octahedral (6).
formulas: Higher bond order → shorter & stronger bond

questions.question: Which of the following has the highest bond order?
questions.explanation: Bond order of O₂⁺ = 2.5 (highest). O₂ = 2, O₂⁻ = 1.5, O₂²⁻ = 1.

questions.question: Which of the following molecules are polar? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: $H_2O$ (bent) and $NH_3$ (pyramidal) are polar due to asymmetric geometry. $CO_2$ (linear) and $CCl_4$ (tetrahedral) are non-polar.

questions.question: How many bonding electrons does the O2 molecule have in its molecular orbital diagram? (Total bonding electrons, Nb)
questions.explanation: O2 has 8 bonding electrons (σ1s², σ*1s² cancel out core; valence: σ2s²,σ*2s² cancel, leaving σ2p² + π2p⁴ = 6 bonding + ... standard MOT gives Nb=8 for O2's valence bonding electrons.

concept.title: Gibbs Free Energy & Spontaneity
body: Gibbs Free Energy combines enthalpy and entropy to predict whether a reaction is spontaneous at constant temperature and pressure: $$\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$$
A reaction is spontaneous (favorable) when $\Delta G < 0$, non-spontaneous when $\Delta G > 0$, and at equilibrium when $\Delta G = 0$.

The sign of $\Delta G$ depends on both $\Delta H$ (heat) and $\Delta S$ (disorder) and temperature $T$. Even an endothermic reaction ($\Delta H > 0$) can be spontaneous if $\Delta S$ is sufficiently positive and $T$ is high enough.
At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$, which gives the relation $\Delta S = \Delta H / T$, useful for finding the temperature at which a reaction becomes spontaneous.

questions.question: A reaction is spontaneous when:
questions.explanation: Spontaneity is determined by $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S < 0$.

questions.question: A reaction has $\Delta H = 50\,\text{kJ/mol}$ and $\Delta S = 100\,\text{J/(mol·K)}$. Find the temperature (in K) above which the reaction becomes spontaneous.
questions.explanation: At the boundary, $\Delta G=0 \Rightarrow T = \Delta H/\Delta S = 50000/100 = 500$ K.
